cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out this problem. So the question is to compute 3 times (1 plus 3 times (1 plus 3 times (1 plus 3 times (1 plus 3)))). Hmm, that looks a bit complicated with all the parentheses, but I think if I take it step by step, starting from the innermost part, it should work out. First, let me write down the expression as given: 3(1 + 3(1 + 3(1 + 3(1 + 3)))) Starting from the innermost bracket, which is (1 + 3). Let me compute that first. 1 plus 3 is 4. So replacing that back into the expression, the next layer out would be: 3(1 + 3(1 + 3(1 + 3(4)))) Now, moving one level up, the next part to compute is the 3(4). Let me do that. 3 times 4 is 12. So now the expression becomes: 3(1 + 3(1 + 3(1 + 12))) Now, adding 1 and 12 gives me 13. So replacing that: 3(1 + 3(1 + 3(13))) Next, compute the 3 times 13. 3 times 13 is 39. Now the expression is: 3(1 + 3(1 + 39)) Adding 1 and 39 gives 40. So now: 3(1 + 3(40)) Calculating 3 times 40: 3 * 40 is 120. Then the expression becomes: 3(1 + 120) Adding 1 and 120 gives 121. Now, the final step is multiplying by 3: 3 * 121. Let me compute that. 3 times 120 is 360, and 3 times 1 is 3, so 360 + 3 = 363.
